planting.note: Root stem tip cuttings under high humidity or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in bright indirect light or full shade after frost; zebra plant is extremely demanding of high humidity (above 60%), warm temperatures, and consistent moisture — drought causes rapid leaf drop, and direct sun scorches the leaves severely.

directions: Root stem tip cuttings or plant container specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in bright indirect light or full shade; zebra plant is a Brazilian tropical evergreen perennial of the family Acanthaceae prized for both its dramatic foliage and its unique flower display — the large, bold, dark-green, glossy, oval leaves are boldly striped with ivory-white midrib and lateral veins in a precise, high-contrast pattern that exactly resembles a zebra's stripes, creating an intensely graphic, formal appearance that makes it immediately recognizable. In addition to this year-round foliage display, zebra plant produces an upright, bright-yellow to golden, pagoda-like flower spike — a dense, overlapping stack of yellow-green bracts from which small, tubular, yellow flowers emerge one or two at a time — that lasts for 4–6 weeks and is quite decorative. As a tropical shade plant, it requires genuinely high humidity, warmth, and consistent moisture; it is challenging to maintain in excellent condition and is best treated as a temporary specimen brought to peak condition for seasonal display rather than a long-term evergreen. One of the more demanding but rewarding tropical foliage plants for the shaded garden.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings under high humidity or plant container specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade after frost; nerve plant requires the highest consistent humidity of virtually any common tropical foliage plant and fails rapidly in dry air — it is ideally suited to terrariums and glass enclosures that maintain near-100% relative humidity.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in full shade; nerve plant (mosaic plant, painted net leaf) is a Peruvian tropical creeping groundcover of the family Acanthaceae, producing small, oval, intensely patterned leaves in which a dense, intricate network of bright-white, pink, or red veins forms a complete lacework overlay on the dark-green leaf surface, creating a mosaic-like pattern that gives the plant its alternative common name. The leaf patterning is extraordinarily precise and complex — the entire leaf surface is filled edge-to-edge with this fine vein network, with no undecorated green area visible. As a creeping, mat-forming groundcover it spreads horizontally across the soil surface to create a dense, living carpet of patterned leaves in terrarium and deep-shade garden conditions. Fittonia is among the most humidity-demanding of all tropical foliage plants — it wilts dramatically at the first sign of dry air, then recovers equally dramatically when humidity is restored. This complete intolerance of dry conditions makes it ideal for terrariums and glass cases but challenging in open interior environments. Pink-veined ('Argyroneura'), red-veined ('Rubra'), and white-veined forms are the primary selections.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings (with at least two nodes) in moist perlite or sphagnum moss, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to average soil in partial shade; wax plant requires a period of cool, dry dormancy in winter (reduced water, no fertilizer, temperatures to 50°F) to initiate the following season's flower buds — plants kept warm and wet year-round often refuse to bloom.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, lean soil in partial shade; wax plant (porcelain flower) is an East Asian tropical vining succulent of the milkweed family (Apocynaceae) that produces clusters of utterly distinctive, perfectly formed, waxy flowers — each cluster a spherical umbel of 20–40 small, star-shaped, pale-pink or white flowers, each flower centered with a smaller red-pink, five-pointed, star-shaped corona that gives the cluster an intricate, jewel-like appearance, and the entire flower cluster glistening with sweet, clear nectar droplets. The flowers are intensely fragrant, especially in the evening. The thick, succulent, oval, glossy, dark-green leaves on the twining stems provide attractive year-round foliage coverage. Flowering requires patience — established plants may take several years to bloom — and a cool, dry winter rest period (the opposite of most tropical plants) to initiate the process. Once blooming age is reached, it flowers reliably from the same spur-like peduncles that should never be removed. 'Krimson Queen' (white-cream leaf margins), 'Tricolor', and the double-flowered 'Compacta' are popular cultivars. A long-lived, low-maintenance succulent vine once established.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; firespike is one of the finest partial-shade hummingbird plants for the Gulf Coast — it blooms most heavily in fall (September–December), providing critical nectar for migrating ruby-throated hummingbirds when few other hummingbird plants are in flower.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; firespike is a Central American tropical shrub of the Acanthaceae family that provides one of the finest fall-season hummingbird plant experiences available to Gulf Coast gardeners — from September through December, when most summer-blooming hummingbird plants have finished, firespike erupts into long, upright, brilliant scarlet-red, tubular flower spikes at the tips of each stem, creating a mass of vivid red color at exactly the time when migrating ruby-throated hummingbirds are moving through the Gulf Coast on their southward migration and actively seeking high-calorie nectar sources. The plants bloom prolifically in fall and the flowering continues until frost kills the stems; in zone 9 the roots survive and regrow reliably in spring, and in zone 10–12 firespike is a permanent, multi-season evergreen perennial. It tolerates partial shade — one of its most valuable attributes — blooming acceptably in the dappled shade of open tree canopies where most hummingbird plants would fail to flower. The glossy, dark-green, oval leaves are attractive year-round. An absolutely essential plant for Gulf Coast wildlife gardens.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; strawberry guava is remarkably adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and tolerates periodic drought, clay, sand, salt spray, and partial shade — one of the most site-tolerant small fruit trees for the Gulf Coast region.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; strawberry guava (cattley guava, cherry guava, purple guava) is a Brazilian evergreen small tree or large shrub that produces small, round, dark-red to burgundy-purple fruits about 1 inch in diameter with a distinctive strawberry-guava flavor — sweet, aromatic, and mildly acidic — that are excellent eaten fresh, made into jams, jellies, juice, and desserts, or dried. The fruits ripen from late summer through fall and fall when fully ripe, making harvest simple. The plant is one of the most attractive year-round fruiting shrubs for zone 9–11, with smooth, mottled, tan and brown peeling bark, glossy, thick, oval, dark-green leaves, and small, white, fragrant guava flowers that precede the fruit. Hardy to approximately 22°F, it is more cold-tolerant than tropical guava (Psidium guajava). However, strawberry guava is listed as a highly invasive species in Hawaii and parts of Florida, where it spreads rapidly in natural areas, and should not be planted near natural habitats in those regions. In drier Gulf Coast areas it is better behaved and is a productive, attractive, low-maintenance small fruit tree.
